title: From headlines to hard data: mapping the uneven impact of geopolitical risk in Europe
summary: The ECB developed a news-based geopolitical risk indicator using domestic EU news sources to measure country-level risk, addressing a gap in US-centric approaches. Russia's February 2022 invasion of Ukraine represented the largest perceived geopolitical shock in at least 20 years. Central and Eastern European (CEE) countries experienced the most severe impacts due to proximity to conflict, energy dependence, and pre-war trade ties with Russia.
key_points: ECB constructed a new geopolitical risk indicator based on keyword searches of leading EU newspapers and news agencies, computing monthly share of articles referencing adverse geopolitical developments
Russia's February 2022 invasion was the largest perceived geopolitical shock in at least 20 years, compounding inflation and increasing financial market volatility
CEE countries were most affected due to energy-intensive production, high food/energy household spending share, economic openness, and disrupted pre-war trade/financial ties with Russia
Investment in CEE countries fell approximately 3 percentage points due to heightened geopolitical uncertainty raising risk premia and discouraging long-term plans
Geopolitical risk shocks drove inflation higher in 2022-2024, with cost-push effects (supply chain disruption, production costs) outweighing demand-dampening effects

CEE countries showed greater export sensitivity than investment sensitivity to geopolitical shocks, contrasting with euro area patterns, due to integration into global supply chains and reliance on external demand
why_it_matters: The ECB's new indicator provides a domestically anchored, EU-wide measure of geopolitical risk that can help policymakers and central banks assess economic vulnerabilities across countries in real time. Understanding the heterogeneous impact of geopolitical shocks is critical for calibrating monetary policy responses and supporting economic resilience across Europe.
macro_context: The article models geopolitical risk shocks using VAR models with variables including real GDP, real investment, real exports, HICP inflation, VIX, and global energy prices over 2004-2024. The analysis shows geopolitical factors contributed significantly to the 2022-2024 inflation surge across Europe, with CEE countries experiencing disproportionate GDP, investment, and export impacts compared to the euro area average.
market_structure_context: The geopolitical risk indicator tracks media coverage volume rather than direct price data, measuring perceived rather than actual risk. The indicator uses a baseline of 100 for January 2014 to July 2025. CEE countries include Bulgaria, Croatia, Czech Republic, Estonia, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Romania, Slovakia, and Slovenia.
important_numbers: 3 percentage points (investment decline in CEE countries from geopolitical shock)
20 years (analysis horizon of geopolitical shock magnitude)
notes: Source is an authoritative ECB research blog post with robust methodology. Article focuses on macro transmission mechanisms rather than direct asset price movements. The 3 percentage point investment decline refers to cumulative contribution of geopolitical risk shocks over 2022-2024 period, not a single-period shock. VIX and financial volatility are mentioned as model inputs but specific volatility levels are not provided.
